speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 3 of the interview with Nathan Peters, June 9. You spent about how many months in hiding in this barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between 13 and 15 months, somewhere-- 13, 14 months. Really, all of 1943 was like nothing happened. I mean, after my father passed away, we buried him. And everybody-- we were just really worried the first few months he was going to keep us or not keep us. And then it was, like, almost routine. We went out once a week to get food. And all day long we, we, we kept quiet. I'm sure the, the cows downstairs had more room than we had. But we didn't-- and then we started-- we were-- towards the end of '43 was, you know, the radios for what's happening, how far the Germans were. They went to Russia to Stalingrad, and [INAUDIBLE] almost to Moscow, like 18 kilometers or something before Moscow. And then when they-- then they started, the Russians started advancing towards us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we did not have a radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you hear this?

speaker: interviewee
text: But he used to come in like every day, every day and say-- and, and, bring us, bring us up to date. He was a very intelligent man. Very-- he was so very intelligent. And he was a religious guy, but a very modern religion. You know, it's-- he used to came up, come up and, and give us like, you know, they, they did, they did not conquer Moscow. They did not [INAUDIBLE]. And then-- and the second or the third-- the last quarter of 1943, the Russians started moving, moving up, and the Germans started, you know, started retreating.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by, by November, October, November, it was, you know, it was the when, when, when, and when, you know, and all the stuff. When will they come? And it-- so '43 was like a dead issue. I mean, it was like, you live like an animal. You didn't know anything else.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just survival, you know, sleep, get up, and then, you know-- and then, when it came to New Years of the end of '43, [INAUDIBLE] was a lot of noise. The Russians are coming back. They're coming back. And the-- But then for some reason they stopped, you know, and it took, like-- but finally in February, they, they passed us through. And they stopped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they stopped in that little village where we used to get our food. And the minute they got there, we, we all went, picked up, and went to that village.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told us. I mean, the guy-- the Russians are here, the-- they're here. And they're, and they're right there, and they're-- and the army's there, and the whole thing. So we picked up, and we went to the guy that used to give us the food. And they were right in the same town. In fact, they were in his house. Because they, they took his house for some command or something. And they treated us very good. Can't complain, and we knew they were-- they had to leave, too, because it was the front line. And they gave us an escort at that time and took us to Luck, to that city. And when we got to Luck--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, wait, this is the eight of you walking with a Russian-- with Russian soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they put us-- they gave us transportation with horses. And they took us to Luck. When we got to Luck, there was some semblance of our Jewish organization. They, they put you up, you know. And we went to their office, and there was a lot of bombed-out places, so they gave us an apartment. And we stayed there a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any possessions with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some carried some stuff, you know, food that a guy gave us, and very little possessions, very little possessions. And we got, we got to Luck, and they gave us an apartment, which was more than we had, we had for the last two years. But the front line started to retreat, and it was very big commotions. I mean, are they going retreat behind Luck or they going to hold them? I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear fighting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, yeah. There, there was-- you could hear with the fighting going on was just a few kilometers out of Luck. Because they were afraid-- because the Russians, at that moment, started to retreat again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it sound like? What did it look like, what you could see?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while, you get so used to it. And once you know that there's really, it's really [INAUDIBLE], you know. If it's-- after a while, you get used to-- it sounded like they were already in town. That's how it sounded. But they were not, by the way. But we didn't take any chances. So most of us, I mean, not only our family, but a lot of Jews, you know, started going through Rowne, because that was further, further away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which way is Rowne compared to Luck? East, west, north, south?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's east. It's-- it's east. Well, directly--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were retreating from the Nazis--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Nazis--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and heading deeper into the Russian--

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian. But, yeah. It was Russian. We knew they weren't going all the way back to, to Moscow, so we retreated to Rowne. And we walked all the way, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how far was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's got to be 60, 70 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall how many days it took you to walk like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, partly we walked, and partly the Russian soldiers used to pick us up and drop us off, you know. It took, it took us three days to get there. Between riding, it wasn't bad. It took us three days to get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you eat along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did really bad, I should say. But you know, the people along the way, they, they were very good to-- at that time, we were not as Jews already. We were just retreating because the Germans were coming back. So they, they'll feed you. They'll put us up. People were nice. They put us up overnight, you know, on the floor. And the-- all of them suffered, so they-- there was very-- a lot of compassion. We got, we got to Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what month of the year was this, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- it, it, it had to be before the, before the war ended, so it had to be something like March.

speaker: interviewer
text: March of '44 or '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. It was still in '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the-- March or maybe April of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was still cold, early spring.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- oh, yeah. And the, the war was still going on. I mean, the war was still at the-- when we got to Rowne, we got an apartment somehow there. We got-- we did get an apartment, and all of us still stayed together, all eight of us. But what happened while this was going on-- and Rowne is in a key link with-- to supply the front line for the Russians. So they bombed us, I want you to know, for six weeks, every single night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? What did you do when they--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, that was the worst part probably, as a child, to remember every single night they lit up the skies and bombed us. In fact, we, we used to run for the basement [INAUDIBLE]. And, and one night-- and we had a church right across the street. And one night-- initially, a lot of people were killed, because they ran out to the streets. And they used to get killed from the shrapnels, you know, just from the pieces. And one night, a bomb came in to part of the church and into the basement we were, but it didn't explode.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw the bomb?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it honestly didn't explode.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they're monsters. It didn't explode. And since then, I want you to know, I never went in another basement. [INAUDIBLE]. Used to sleep upstairs. The windows, the windows come out. And this lasted about six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, you, you wouldn't, when the-- even when the bombs came, you would stay upstairs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stay upstairs because it doesn't pay. They said-- after a while, it's really-- now, why would you-- OK, get down, get down. Because me and my cousin, we always talk about-- says, you know, they used to run to this cellar. We [INAUDIBLE], no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the windows break from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, windows were gone. I mean, like, it's unbelievable. And the following day, they took it out. They dismantled it. [INAUDIBLE] But we had the worst time as the six weeks in Rowne, where the, where the bombing, the German bombing. And then as soon the front line moved away, it became normal. I mean, normal that, you know, nobody fired at you. And they set up the soup kitchens and organized. I mean, somehow we was organized pretty good. And maybe there's a black market in [INAUDIBLE], you know, and-- from the, from the Russian soldiers who sold them vodka. My mother was baking rolls and selling them in the markets to survive. And my brother went to the army. He was in the-- he went into the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how old was he at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, this-- he went to the army at the end of '44, so he was, like, 19, I guess. He wasn't even-- he was, he was born 1925, so he was 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: He really-- they probably would have drafted him, but he volunteered.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: He did volunteer. But the-- we all thought it was nuts, but he did volunteer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think he wanted to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he wanted to get away from all this stuff. It's like, you know, being, being able to do something and maybe want to get revenge on the Germans. But I think it's like, you know, being penned up for all these years-- you're talking about from '41-- I mean, now they said it was '44 was three years longer. I think he just, like, wanted to go out in the world a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what his experiences were with the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went all the way to Konigsberg and then was wounded, and they sent him back in-- before the war ended. But he was wounded. He had a leg wound. They sent him back to somewhere past Moscow. But he went all the way to Germany. He was wounded, and then we brought him home in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what I mean by brought him home, because they wouldn't release him so fast, so my mother and my sister made a trip out there, paid somebody off, and got him out. Because-- in the, the most that we want to get him out because we wanted to leave Russia, and we didn't want to leave him there. It was at that time, they came out if you were under Polish denomination before '39, and if you wanted to go to Poland, you can do that. So I wasn't going to go without him, right? So you fit that category? You had permission to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. But we had to get in. So we went out there and--

speaker: interviewer
text: By train, they went, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, by train. It's the only way through there. And they paid off to get him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he, was he badly wounded or was-- how was he by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was-- he had some discomfort, you know, after [INAUDIBLE]. It probably wasn't healed right. But no, he was-- he just, he had a leg wound, you know. He just-- he was in a hospital, and then he came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he decorated with a medal for--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- so how was it-- do you remember the day he came back with your mother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: He, he, he had to come back, probably, of June of-- June of '45, something like that, because we waited for him to be able to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember-- can you picture what it was like? Can you describe it to me when he, he and your mother and sister came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] first of all, we never-- we, we told him it was never going to happen. You can't buy modern Russian army, right? And-- because first of all, they went on false papers. My mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother and--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and sister, because you could never get permission to go, to go that far. So they went on false papers completely, you know. And [INAUDIBLE] figure you're going to get killed. You're going to get arrested before you get them. But he came back. It was, it was really, at that time, you know, like the, the biggest thing happening-- get him out-- for them to come back. Because, I mean, they had papers. Somebody picked them up, that was probably 10 years in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they left, you thought you might not see them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Because you figure, you know, they pick you up. I mean, we knew it was false papers. I mean, there was no question about it, was standing about the--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were now 15 years-- only 15?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, I was almost 15, yeah. And they brought him back. They brought him back. And it was-- and we didn't really hang out very long. We got the papers, and we moved. And we, we moved to, I think-- moved to Bytom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Bytom?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's in Poland. It's B-I-D-O-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you go back to Ostrozec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, during that time, yeah. We went back to Ostrozec during that time. We-- first of all. I went back to, to Ostrozec twice. First, I went back to see what happened. And that was actually before the, before the war, the war ended. So I went back to see what happened. And the whole town was dismantled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you went by yourself or with your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with my cousin. First, I went with my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were two, like, 13-, 14-year-olds?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- yeah. We went-- in fact, we went to visit a guy that saved us. It sounds funny. And he gave us two horses back-- and we went to the town, and then we came back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see in Ostrozec when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole town was dismantled. Absolutely all the homes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Burned down or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, dismantled. Really, they sold the-- the way I understand it happened, they sold the houses to the farmers, and they dismantled them. And there must have been around six, seven homes, and our home was there. And, and, and who was there? The Russian police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Living in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Living-- not living. They had it as a, as a police station. And they, and they let us go in and look around and everything. But you really couldn't live there because it was like, there's nobody there. I mean, there was not one person, one Jew there. I mean, no-- nobody lived there. And then we--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the barn next door that your aunts and cousins lived in? Was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was taken apart brick by brick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing left. It was really-- it was amazing to see it, like all these wooden homes and, you know-- with straw roofs, and nothing left. About six homes, five, six homes, that was the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: About-- do you have a-- was it a, a Russian army base at this time, or just a little--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a police station. The army--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a little police station?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no army. It was a police station.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you took the horses and you went back to the farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Went back there. And then, then we went back by-- yeah. We used to do a lot of hitchhiking. In 1945, to get from place to place, the trains would take you for the army. Everything-- you did a lot of hitchhiking, but the, the Russian army, they'll pick you up from place to place. So we went back there, and then we begged to-- and went to a small place in [NON-ENGLISH]. And then we used to hitchhike, used to hitchhike a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother feel about you going off, back to Ostrozec at that, that first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't-- I, I don't think she was real worried about it, you know. You go through so much that you really don't think the dangers. They really don't think of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did--

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was a lot of danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: The war was still going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was still going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were behind the Russian--

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was so far away. It was so-- the war was already in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already out of Poland. It was already in Germany, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hope to see when you got back to Ostrozec that first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we really wanted to see what happened. What happened? And it's like curiosity. And is there anybody there? There was nobody. There was nobody there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went back-- then you were-- you were in Rowne, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then we went back a second time.

speaker: interviewer
text: From when you were in Bytom or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, from, from Rowne. Once we, once we left Rowne, that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So why did you go back the second time from Rowne?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- I went with my mother at that time. Yeah, me and my mother went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: She wanted to see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wanted to see it. And then we hid some-- before we left, we, we hid some Russian gold rubles in the basement. And I said to her-- and I, I was the guy that hid it, by the way. Because I was a very trusted young kid. So I said, no, we go back, we can take it out. And she wanted to see-- she, she couldn't believe what happened to Ostrozec. So we went back, and the police station was still there. They let us in again. They were very cordial to us. We went into the basement, and she chickened out. She says, you're not going to do that. She says, you're going to get arrested if you're going to do that. We didn't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's still there. So it's still there. It's still there, the, the Russian-- gold and Russian rubles are still there. And that was the last time I was there. I did go to-- I did go to Russia in '89, [INAUDIBLE], but I never went all the way there. I went over to Kiev, but I didn't go any, any further west.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how far is Ostrozec from Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately under 200 miles. I would say probably 160 miles, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you went back the second time with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she wouldn't let--

speaker: interviewer
text: She wouldn't dig up the gold.

speaker: interviewee
text: She wouldn't let me do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went back to Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Rowne and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we left. We went to Bytom, so that was already in '45, and the war was already ended. That was-- it was-- I believe we got there, it was in June. The war was already ended, and they were very well organized, you know. The, the [NON-ENGLISH] was there, we know, and not officially. They're very organized, and they really shuffled people where to go. And we, we stayed there a short period of time, probably, maybe two months, maybe not. And we-- they used to shuffle from place to place, like we went to Czechoslovakia first. And then we, we supposedly went as Greek-- Greeks coming home. It was really, you know, like-- and then from there, we went to Czechoslovakia, and then we went to Austria, and from Austria, we want to Munich. All-- they were leaving. I mean, we were just like a cow being led, you know. They, they-- everything was arranged. And there was the [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish organization that officially, obviously, they couldn't do that. And we got all the way to Munich in 1945 before Christmas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when we spoke on the phone, you mentioned a place called Katowice. Katowice? What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Bytom. I really gave you the wrong name. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's Bytom. Would that count as a DP camp or no? That was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no DP camp after that the first camp. So we got to Munich, and then, and then the-- and it was really a lot of-- a, a lot of Jews came to Munich at the time. Then when the DP camps were created by the UNNRA, I think, the United-- not the United Nations, United something. They created UNNRA, U-N-N-R-A. And the DP camps were created. First, so in the [INAUDIBLE], we stayed in the, the Munich Museum. It was bombed down, but we stayed in the Munich Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there still art there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no art there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just big rooms, big empty rooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Big empty rooms. And the-- in Austria, we stayed in [INAUDIBLE] hospital was half bombed-out, too. So in Munich, they, they really started resettling the people. First, they sent us to a small town. The name was [NON-ENGLISH], but we didn't like it. We came back and said we'd rather go to the DP camp. We went to Fohrenwald. And we stayed in Fohrenwald. I mean, it was really organized. I mean, there was enough food. I mean, there was shelter. Obviously, it was a barracks before. And it, it was a big camp. Fohrenwald was one of the biggest DP camps. They had Regensburg. But the-- but Fohrenwald was a big, very well run DP camp. And people were waiting. A lot of people went to Israel before Israel became a state. A lot of people want to Norway. I mean, they're [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how many people were in Fohrenwald when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it had to be well over 2,000, 3,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where had most of them been during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had been in--

speaker: interviewee
text: All survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in camps, in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: In camps, in hiding, all, all people leaving Russia, Poland, and trying to move west. I mean, all the way, even South America, anyplace. Everybody was moving someplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it to be in Germany after the Germans had been the source of so much misery for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people that I, that I, that we associate, nobody wanted to stay there. But, you know, they were really pretty well-- Munich was bombed out. And the Germans were really, you know-- you would like to see them struggle, because they really struggled. I mean, they had no food, and bombed out, and no, and no shelter. You can see Munich today, but you had to see it in '45 or-- it was like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were just hoping that they, they would never fix it up, but obviously the Marshall Plan did a pretty good job on that one. And then we, and then we were there-- I was there till '47. And while we're there, we went to school at a Hebrew school, which I have a picture of it. And it was normal, very normal life. You know, you went to school. After school, you had facilities, you know, gymnasiums if you wanted to play ball. We had a soccer team. It was very, very, very organized. But we all know it was temporary. You know, we got to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you living with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we-- I lived-- my cousin, my cousin was not with us no more. He, he went to, actually, to a training camp in the area not far away. He went, he went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was only about 16?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, 16, that's right. He went there. He didn't want to-- he did not come there with us. My-- the rest of us were together, except, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] except for the, the person that survived with us, which I still can't think of her name right now. She did not go with us. She went to a different place, but also to a DP camp. She also went to the DP camp, but she didn't stay with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was like a little-- a few months of normal childhood for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was normal. Yeah, it was two years, almost two years. Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] It was very normal. You went to school. And we did some black market, you know, if you wanted a little luxury. Which I was arrested, so one weekend I stayed in jail in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Frankfurt, yeah. Well, we, we smuggled some, believe it or not, it's schmaltz, some fat, you know, from, from Munich to Frankfurt. And it was very-- so I used to do it on the weekends. I was picked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, well, well, how did you do it? What did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We bought it in-- we bought it where we were. And we used to go on the train and go to Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to sell it in Frankfurt--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sell it, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --because they didn't have enough there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was more expensive there. And also salami, this type. And it was, you know, to get some luxuries, and-- so one weekend, I, I, I was picked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with another guy, but I was the only guy picked up, because they just like, you know-- and I spent July 4th weekend in a, in a Frankfurt jail, which was not very pleasant, by the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did they pick you up with, with black market goods on you? Is that what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's, that's the point.

speaker: interviewer
text: The luxuries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as a Jew. It was just black market, yes. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the jail like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jail was terrible. I mean, I think that's the only time I've ever spent a day, but it's a two by four. It was with another person there, and I got stuck with July 4th weekend. The way I got released is they really took me to, to American, not, not to German court. And I really gave them some story, which really made no sense, and the guy really couldn't care less, and he dismissed the case, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your, your mother must have been so frightened when you didn't come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- just about getting [INAUDIBLE]. You know what I mean? Then when I got out, because I was supposed to be back the day before, but they really didn't know when to look for me. But by the time I got out and I called them, it was-- it was an extra experience which I could have lived without.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did you continue to smuggle after that or did that finish it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That was really the end of it. It was really getting too close for us to leave. So my cousin-- let's see, he went, he went to go to Israel. I'll be honest with you, maybe I shouldn't say, but I didn't want to go. I said, I just have enough of war, and I really don't want to go to Israel right now. He said, you're, you're crazy. All these years, we're all together. I said, Aaron, I really got to hang out longer. He didn't. He left before-- he left in '47, and he went to Israel. And he got to Israel-- different story. He got to Israel before Israel was a state.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in Aliyah Bet? Was he brought in illegally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Illegally, yes. He came illegally, and he's a year older than I am, and he served in every war, '48, '56, '73, and, and '67. And want you know, he never got a scratch. Could not believe that. He was here two years ago, and never got a scratch. He has-- and now he has, he has three kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does he live in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in a kibbutz, [NON-ENGLISH]. And that's-- he's a very happy guy. He got off the kibbutz once. He didn't like it, and he went back. But none of his kids are in kibbutz anymore. They're all off the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been there to visit him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've been to Israel probably six, seven times. The last time I was there was when his youngest son got married. But I-- he was-- yeah, he was here twice. I brought him here twice. Last time I brought him was for my granddaughter's bar mitzvah. And that's when I made my, my, my bar mitzvah, so I brought him up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in '47, we couldn't-- it was getting itchy. Now, when are we going to get out of Germany? Got to leave Germany, new [INAUDIBLE] state. Obviously, everybody wanted to go to America, United States. It didn't look that we have anybody that would be able to do that. You, you had an aunt here. She couldn't help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow, I really don't-- either, either they weren't willing-- I really don't want to, you know, say anything. She's not alive. May she rest in peace. Somehow, we didn't get any papers to go. And there was no-- didn't look like [INAUDIBLE]. You know, we-- sooner or later, after Israel, I guess everybody knows you're going to wind up in Israel. So when the Canadian Jewish congress came out that you can-- they would pick everybody up, anybody under 18 with no parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it was again in Europe, they have papers, very easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother wasn't superstitious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It, it was just to get out, you know, get, get out of Germany, get out of Germany. And got my paper, that I was, you know [INAUDIBLE], and I went, and I went to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: By yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: By myself, in, in June of '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many kids-- how many young people traveled with you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's a lot of people. And I, I have some of the pictures, too, that I'll show you. We really had a boat full of people. I would say a couple thousand.

speaker: interviewer
text: All young people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody under 18. You had to be under 18. You [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they bring counselors, chaperones?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had a guide. Yeah, we had a guide who was in charge of the whole, the whole thing. And, and they split us up into groups, you know, on the boats. These are going to Montreal. They're going to Toronto. They're going to Winnipeg. You know, it's-- it was [INAUDIBLE]. And somehow I was the one that went to Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many were in your group that went to Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: 20, 25, maybe. A, a lot of--

speaker: interviewer
text: All about your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the same age. They had to be within, you know, under, under 18. Maybe a few of them made themselves younger, you know, but there, there were not too many over 20. It was a pretty good time in Montreal. I mean, you know, we went-- we went-- I went to work, and I went to night, night school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They set, they set us up with, with families, with Jewish families. And they paid for the room. And they set up each one with, with a different family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you stay with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with, I stayed with a family in-- [INAUDIBLE], I think it was. And my-- and I stayed there not long, though, really. I went on my own. I really didn't want to live this way, so I-- I had a-- one of my friends and myself, we went out on our own, so we moved away from the families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thought it was Lieberman [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were just with them about how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably about four or five months. And then myself and a friend, we went on our own, so we got our own place, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your friend's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harry Lieberman. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: This tape is about to end. And then we'll continue the story in the next tape.
